perturbation.original_text: Notice given by mail shall be considered to have been given five (5) days after the mailing thereof, and notices given by facsimile shall be considered to have been given when received.
perturbation.changed_text: Notice given by mail shall be considered to have been given one (1) day after the mailing thereof, and notices given by facsimile shall be considered to have been given when received.
perturbation.explanation: Shortening the mail notice period to one day may not allow sufficient time for the recipient to act, especially given potential mail delays, conflicting with standards of reasonable notice.
perturbation.law_explanation: Connecticut's Unfair Trade Practices Act requires fair and reasonable business practices. Reducing the notice period to one day for mailed notices could be deemed unfair or deceptive if it doesn't provide adequate opportunity for the recipient to respond, especially concerning important contractual matters.

(here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Connecticut General Statutes Title 42 - Business, Selling, Trading and Collection Practices Chapter 735a - Unfair Trade Practices Section 42-110b. - Unfair trade practices prohibited. Legislative intent. Universal Citation: CT Gen Stat § 42-110b. (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next (a) No person shall engage in unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing subsection (a) of this section, the commissioner and the courts of this state shall be guided by interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the federal courts to Section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 USC 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The commissioner may, in accordance with chapter 54, establish by regulation acts, practices or methods which shall be deemed to be unfair or deceptive in violation of subsection (a) of this section. Such regulations shall not be inconsistent with the rules, regulations and decisions of the federal trade commission and the federal courts in interpreting the provisions of the Federal Trade Commission Act. (d) It is the intention of the legislature that this chapter be remedial and be so construed. (P.A. 73-615, S. 2, 16; P.A. 75-618, S. 1, 11; P.A. 76-303, S. 1, 4.) History; P.A. 75-618 rephrased provisions for clarity and consistency; P.A. 76-303 deleted provision in Subsec. (a) which established unfa
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the mail notice period from five days to one day. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this may not allow sufficient time for the recipient to act, potentially conflicting with standards of reasonable notice. The Law Explanation connects this to Connecticut's Unfair Trade Practices Act, which requires fair and reasonable business practices. Scraped Law Snippet 2, specifically CT Gen Stat § 42-110b (a), states that "No person shall engage in unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce." Reducing the notice period to one day could be considered an unfair or deceptive practice because it may not provide adequate opportunity for response, thus contradicting the law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be terminated by either party upon thirty (30) days written notice to the other. Unless thus earlier terminated, this Agreement shall terminate upon completion of the Pilot Study. The confidentiality obligations under this Agreement will survive termination of this Agreement for a period of three (3) years.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be terminated by either party upon ten (10) days written notice to the other. Unless thus earlier terminated, this Agreement shall terminate upon completion of the Pilot Study. The confidentiality obligations under this Agreement will survive termination of this Agreement for a period of one (1) year.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice to 10 days and the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect Yale's proprietary research data related to the Pilot Study, potentially violating its rights.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Connecticut Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Connecticut General Statutes § 35-51
perturbation.law_explanation: Under the Connecticut Uniform Trade Secrets Act, misappropriation can occur even after a short time if it leads to competitive advantage.  A one-year confidentiality period may be insufficient to prevent ABP from using Yale's confidential information to develop competing products, thereby undermining the purpose of trade secret protection.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract reduces the confidentiality period from three years to one year and the termination notice from 30 days to 10 days. The Law Explanation states that under the Connecticut Uniform Trade Secrets Act, a one-year confidentiality period may be insufficient to prevent ABP from using Yale's confidential information to develop competing products, thus undermining trade secret protection. This indicates a significant weakening of protection compared to the original agreement, and therefore creates a legal contradiction based on the inconsistency perturbation type.

perturbation.original_text: All notices, instructions, requests, authorizations, consents, demands and other communications hereunder shall be in writing and shall be sent by facsimile, overnight courier or registered airmail, postage prepaid, addressed as follows: For Pilot Site: Yale Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery Department of Surgery Yale School of Medicine 333 Cedar Street (BB-330) P.O. Box 208041 New Haven, Connecticut 06520-8041 Attention: Dr. Yuan Liu/Dr. John Persing Facsimile: 203-785-5714
perturbation.changed_text: All notices, instructions, requests, authorizations, consents, demands and other communications hereunder shall be in writing and shall be sent by regular mail, addressed as follows: For Pilot Site: Yale Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery Department of Surgery Yale School of Medicine 333 Cedar Street (BB-330) P.O. Box 208041 New Haven, Connecticut 06520-8041 Attention: Dr. Yuan Liu/Dr. John Persing
perturbation.explanation: Removing options for expedited communication methods may violate reasonable standards of business practice, particularly when time-sensitive information is crucial to the Pilot Study.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing (Connecticut Law)
perturbation.law_citation: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, which is part of Connecticut contract law, requires parties to act honestly and reasonably in the performance and enforcement of contracts. Eliminating expedited methods for notices may violate this covenant if it demonstrably hinders Yale's ability to effectively participate in the Pilot Study or respond to important issues arising from it.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes options for expedited communication (facsimile, overnight courier, registered airmail) and only allows regular mail. The Law Explanation states this could violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Connecticut contract law if it hinders Yale's ability to effectively participate in the Pilot Study. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'good faith' as encompassing 'honest dealing' and requiring 'faithful performance of duties'. Removing expedited communication methods could be seen as a failure to act in good faith if it demonstrably impairs Yale's responsiveness to important issues, thus contradicting the duty of good faith.

perturbation.original_text: Proprietary Information shall be designated as such in writing by the Disclosing Party by appropriate legend or stamp, or, if first disclosed in a non-written or other non-tangible form, Proprietary Information shall be identified by the Disclosing Party at the time of disclosure as being disclosed in confidence and shall be reduced to tangible form and marked as confidential or proprietary and such tangible form shall be delivered to the Receiving Party within thirty (30) days after the date of first disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Proprietary Information shall be designated as such in writing by the Disclosing Party by appropriate legend or stamp, or, if first disclosed in a non-written or other non-tangible form, Proprietary Information shall be identified by the Disclosing Party at their convenience.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the thirty (30) day deadline for reducing non-written information to tangible form removes a level of security and accountability. Allowing the information to be reduced at 'their convenience' could lead to trade secret misappropriation, particularly if not reduced to tangible form quickly.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Connecticut Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Connecticut General Statutes § 35-51
perturbation.law_explanation: The Connecticut Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Removing the deadline for reducing non-written disclosures to tangible form can weaken the effort to maintain its secrecy, and would therefore weaken the parties' rights under the act.

Cuomo) State Regulations Toolbox about
perturbation.justification: The change introduces a legal contradiction. The original contract language requires Proprietary Information disclosed in a non-written form to be reduced to tangible form and delivered to the Receiving Party within thirty (30) days. This is a time-sensitive obligation. The modified language changes this obligation, replacing the deadline with 'at their convenience'. The Connecticut Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets, according to the Law Explanation. Removing the 30-day deadline significantly weakens the effort to maintain the secrecy of the information. The new language provides no specific deadline for converting proprietary information into tangible form, which directly contradicts the requirement to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. The scraped snippet 1 does not provide any usable information.
